title: Frontend with HTMX, Alpine.js and daisyUI
description: Build a server-rendered interactive frontend for your FastAPI app. HTMX handles dynamic updates without writing JavaScript, daisyUI provides styled components, and Alpine.js manages client-side state.

steps.title: The stack: HTMX, daisyUI and Alpine.js
steps.instruction:
## The stack

Instead of building a JavaScript SPA (React, Vue, Svelte), this approach keeps logic on the server and sends back HTML instead of JSON.

**HTMX** replaces the need for JavaScript `fetch()` calls. Add `hx-get`, `hx-post`, or `hx-delete` to any HTML element and HTMX fires the request and swaps the response into the page automatically.

**daisyUI** is a component library built on top of Tailwind CSS. It gives you classes like `btn`, `card`, `input`, and `badge` so you get great-looking UI without writing custom CSS.

**Alpine.js** fills in the gaps: local UI state that does not need a server round-trip, like toggling a dropdown or filtering a list client-side.

All four vendor files are served from `/vendor/` so the app runs fully offline.

## Task

Create the base HTML template. Add all four vendor includes and a daisyUI navbar.

```html
<script src="/vendor/tailwind.cdn.js"></script>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="/vendor/daisyui.min.css">
<script defer src="/vendor/alpine.min.js"></script>
<script src="/vendor/htmx.min.js"></script>
```

Set `data-theme="dark"` on `<html>` to use daisyUI's dark theme.
steps.starter_code:
Todos
{% block content %}{% endblock %}
steps.solution_code:
Todos
{% block content %}{% endblock %}
steps.hint: Add the four script and link tags inside the <head> element. Order matters: Tailwind first, daisyUI CSS second, Alpine.js with defer third, HTMX last.

steps.title: Building the layout with daisyUI
steps.instruction:
## daisyUI components

daisyUI adds semantic class names on top of Tailwind. You do not write custom CSS at all.

Useful classes for this project:

| Class | What it does |
|---|---|
| `card` + `card-body` | A raised content panel |
| `btn btn-primary` | A styled primary button |
| `btn btn-error btn-sm` | A small red button |
| `input input-bordered` | A styled text input |
| `badge badge-ghost` | A small pill label |
| `loading loading-spinner` | An animated CSS spinner |

Components inherit their colors from the active `data-theme`, so everything adjusts automatically when you switch themes.

## Task

Create `templates/index.html`. It extends `base.html` and uses a daisyUI card to hold a todo form and a placeholder list container.

Give the list container `id="todo-list"` as HTMX will target this id in later steps.
steps.starter_code:
{% extends "base.html" %} {% block content %}

My Todo List

No todos yet.

{% endblock %}
steps.solution_code:
{% extends "base.html" %} {% block content %}

My Todo List

No todos yet.

{% endblock %}
steps.hint: The key detail is `id="todo-list"` on the container div. HTMX uses this id as the target when loading and updating the list.

steps.title: FastAPI with Jinja2 templates
steps.instruction:
## Returning HTML from FastAPI

By default FastAPI returns JSON. To return HTML you need two things:

**`Jinja2Templates`** renders `.html` files from a folder on disk:
```python
from fastapi.templating import Jinja2Templates
templates = Jinja2Templates(directory="templates")
```

**`TemplateResponse`** renders the template and returns it as an HTTP response:
```python
return templates.TemplateResponse("index.html", {"request": request})
```

The `request` object is always required in the template context. FastAPI needs it to build absolute URLs inside templates.

Also mount the vendor folder as static files so the browser can load them:
```python
from fastapi.staticfiles import StaticFiles
app.mount("/vendor", StaticFiles(directory="static/vendor"), name="vendor")
```

## Task

Create `main.py` with:
- Static file mounting for `/vendor/`
- An in-memory todos list
- `GET /` returning the index page
- `GET /todos` returning a `todos.html` fragment with the todos list
steps.starter_code:
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.templating import Jinja2Templates
from fastapi.staticfiles import StaticFiles
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse

app = FastAPI()

app.mount("/vendor", StaticFiles(directory="static/vendor"), name="vendor")

templates = Jinja2Templates(directory="templates")

# In-memory todo store
todos = []
next_id = 1

@app.get("/", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def index(request: Request):
    # Return the main page
    pass

@app.get("/todos", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def get_todos(request: Request):
    # Return the todo list fragment
    pass
steps.hint: Both routes call templates.TemplateResponse(). The GET / route renders index.html with just the request. The GET /todos route renders todos.html and passes the todos list in the context dict.

steps.solution_code:
{% if todos %}
    {% for todo in todos %}
  • {{ todo.title }}
    • {% endfor %}
{% else %}

No todos yet. Add one above.

{% endif %}
steps.hint: Use `{% for todo in todos %}` inside `{% if todos %}`. Give each `<li>` the attribute `id="todo-{{ todo.id }}"` using double curly braces for the variable.

steps.title: HTMX: load and add todos
steps.instruction:
## HTMX attributes

HTMX turns any HTML element into a request trigger. The core attributes:

| Attribute | What it does |
|---|---|
| `hx-get="/url"` | Fire a GET request to this URL |
| `hx-post="/url"` | Fire a POST when the element is activated |
| `hx-trigger="load"` | Fire automatically when the element loads |
| `hx-target="#id"` | Put the response inside this element |
| `hx-swap="innerHTML"` | Replace the target's inner HTML |

## Wiring up the list

On the `#todo-list` div, add `hx-get="/todos"` and `hx-trigger="load"` to fetch and render todos on page open.

## Wiring up the form

On the `<form>`, add `hx-post="/todos"` to submit via HTMX instead of a normal browser POST. Use the same `hx-target` and `hx-swap` to update the list after adding.

Add `hx-on::after-request="this.reset()"` to the form to clear the input field after each submission.

## Task

Update `templates/index.html` with HTMX attributes on both the list container and the form.
steps.starter_code:
{% extends "base.html" %} {% block content %}

My Todo List

Loading...

{% endblock %}
steps.solution_code:
{% extends "base.html" %} {% block content %}

My Todo List

Loading...

{% endblock %}
steps.hint: Add hx-get, hx-trigger="load", hx-target, and hx-swap to the #todo-list div. Add hx-post, hx-target, hx-swap, and hx-on::after-request to the form.

steps.title: POST and DELETE routes
steps.instruction:
## Receiving form data

When HTMX submits a form, it sends the data as `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` (standard HTML form encoding). FastAPI reads this with `Form(...)`:

```python
from fastapi import Form

@app.post("/todos", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def add_todo(request: Request, title: str = Form(...)):
    ...
```

After adding the todo, return the updated `todos.html` fragment. HTMX will swap it into `#todo-list`.

## DELETE route

The DELETE route removes a todo by id and returns an empty response. Because the template uses `hx-swap="outerHTML"` on each row (you will add that in the next step), an empty response causes the row to disappear:

```python
@app.delete("/todos/{todo_id}", response_class=HTMLResponse)
async def delete_todo(todo_id: int):
    todos[:] = [t for t in todos if t["id"] != todo_id]
    return HTMLResponse(content="")
```

## Task

Add the `POST /todos` and `DELETE /todos/{todo_id}` routes to `main.py`.
steps.hint: Use `global next_id` before modifying it. The POST route appends a new dict and returns the todos.html fragment. The DELETE route filters the list and returns HTMLResponse(content="").

steps.title: HTMX: deleting individual todos
steps.instruction:
## hx-delete and outerHTML swap

Update `todos.html` to wire up the delete button on each row.

Three attributes work together:

- `hx-delete="/todos/{{ todo.id }}"` sends a DELETE request to the route you just created
- `hx-target="#todo-{{ todo.id }}"` targets the `<li>` for this todo
- `hx-swap="outerHTML"` replaces the entire `<li>` (including itself) with the response

The DELETE route returns an empty string, so the `<li>` is replaced with nothing and it disappears from the page. No JavaScript needed.

`hx-confirm` adds a browser confirmation dialog before the request fires:
```html
hx-confirm="Delete this todo?"
```

## Task

Update `templates/todos.html`. Add `hx-delete`, `hx-target`, `hx-swap`, and `hx-confirm` to each delete button.
steps.starter_code:
{% if todos %}
    {% for todo in todos %}
  • {{ todo.title }}
    • {% endfor %}
{% else %}

No todos yet. Add one above.

{% endif %}
steps.solution_code:
{% if todos %}
    {% for todo in todos %}
  • {{ todo.title }}
    • {% endfor %}
{% else %}

No todos yet. Add one above.

{% endif %}
steps.hint: Add hx-delete="/todos/{{ todo.id }}", hx-target="#todo-{{ todo.id }}", hx-swap="outerHTML", and hx-confirm="Delete this todo?" to each delete button.

steps.title: Alpine.js, loading indicator and toast
steps.instruction:
## Alpine.js for local state HTMX handles server round-trips. Alpine.js handles state that lives entirely in the browser. A search filter is a perfect example: filtering the visible list as the user types does not need a server request. Wrap the card in `x-data` and bind the filter input with `x-model`: ```html
``` HTMX and Alpine.js coexist on the same elements with no conflict. ## HTMX loading indicator `hx-indicator` points to an element HTMX will show during a request and hide when done. daisyUI's `loading` class provides a ready-made spinner: ```html ``` Add `hx-indicator="#spinner"` to the form. ## Toast notification Listen for HTMX's `htmx:afterRequest` event with Alpine.js to show a success toast: ```html
Todo saved!
``` ## Task Update `index.html` with the Alpine.js search input, the spinner indicator, and the toast notification.
steps.starter_code:
{% extends "base.html" %} {% block content %}

My Todo List

{% endblock %}
steps.solution_code:
{% extends "base.html" %} {% block content %}
Todo saved!

My Todo List

{% endblock %}
steps.hint: Add the toast div before the card. Wrap the card in x-data="{ search: '' }". Add x-model="search" to a new filter input. Add the spinner span and hx-indicator="#spinner" on the form.
